sections.h:
INTRODUCTION

This manual has been created to provide police officers with an easy to read guide to investigative interviewing of witnesses.

The manual deals with interviewing witnesses of all categories. It is important for the interviewer to identify into which category each witness falls, as not all witnesses should be dealt with in the same manner.

An eyewitness is someone who personally saw or was involved in the incident under investigation. A non-eyewitness, which includes, other significant witness, general witness or an expert witness are witnesses who may not have seen the incident occur but have other information relevant to the inquiry. In all cases the overall purpose of the interview is the same, although the interviewers approach will be slightly different.

Practice in using the Free Recall Model will enhance the natural flexibility of the model, resulting in a free flowing interview style that meets the interviewer's objective.

Related Information

More information on investigative interviewing can be found on the Investigative Standards Intranet Site (Investigative Interviewing page).

sections.h:
DEFINITIONS

To assist officers, a definition of terms used in this manual is shown below.

ADVOKATE Is the mnemonic for:

A – Amount of time under observation.

D – Distance from the witnessed event.

V – Visibility at the time of the event.

O – Obstructions.

K – Known or seen before.

A – Any reason to remember.

T – Time since the incident.

E – Errors or discrepancies.

This mnemonic is specifically for police to capture evidence that fall within each of the above headings when taking statements or conducting interviews.

Automated Interview Plan (AIP): Electronic interview plan used for suspects and witnesses. Located on the WA Police Force Intranet here.

Challenge: Seeking an explanation from the interviewee for inconsistencies between facts known by police and the interviewees account.

Cognitive Interview Model: The cognitive interview is a memory facilitator, with the interviewee being in control of the information flowing from the interview.

Culturally and Linguistically Diverse (CaLD): A person who is vulnerable due to their culture or whose second language is English.

Free Recall Model: A basic interview model which can be used for interviewing witnesses in the majority of situations.

Interviewee: The person who is being interviewed.

Investigating Officer (IO): The officer who is investigating the incident.

Investigative interviewing: The questioning of witnesses and suspects to obtain accurate and reliable information about matters under investigation.

Investigatively Important Topics: Of value to the investigation because it helps to establish the nature and elements of the offence, possible defences, the identity of the offender or further lines of enquiry.

PEACE Model: An interviewing framework that provides a structure to planning and conducting effective investigative interviews.

Physical Material (PM): Items which include DNA, fingerprints, impressions, photographs, clothing, weapons, documents, computer generated data, trace evidence, Closed Circuit Television (CCTV) footage, ballistics, crash reports and all other forensic samples.

Suspect: A person suspected of having committed an offence, whether or not he or she has been charged with the offence.

TEDS: A mnemonic that is helpful when constructing open questions. It stands for: Tell, Explain, Describe, Show.

Victim: A person injured, or suffering loss or harm as a result of an offence.

Vulnerable Person: For the purpose of a police interview, be that suspect or witness, is defined as someone who falls within one or more of the following categories:

• A child is always a vulnerable person.

• People with impaired intellectual function are always vulnerable.

Or people who:

• Have impaired physical functioning.

• Are Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islanders.

• Are from non-English speaking backgrounds.

• Have suffered an emotional or traumatic incident.

Which as a result, may:

• Predispose them to suggestibility.

• Impact on voluntariness.

• Complicate communications between the police and the interviewee.

Any of the above circumstances may result in flawed information being captured during the interview.

Persons fitting this definition must be afforded enhanced services to ensure fairness to the person and accuracy of the information obtained during an interview.

Witness: A person who has information about an alleged offence or offender, even if they were not physically present at the event. Everyone who provides relevant information is a 'witness' so the term includes victim, eyewitness, other significant witness, general witness, expert witness or police witness.

5W&H: A mnemonic that is helpful when constructing open questions. It stands for: Who, What, When, Where, Why and How.

sections.t: Witness interviewing — who is a witness
sections.h:
WITNESS INTERVIEWING

Non-Eyewitness

Some confusion has arisen in the past in relation to non-eyewitness interview techniques. This was largely due to the failure of interviewers to recognise different approaches are required for eyewitness vs non-eyewitnesses. As stated in the introduction to this manual, a non-eyewitness is someone who has not actually witnessed the event but may be able to supply information relating to some other aspect of the matter. A non-eyewitness could be an expert witness providing technical evidence (For example, a bank teller explaining how credit card transactions are processed in a bank). An eyewitness will often be asked to recall the incident as though they were still at the scene but obviously this is not appropriate for a person who is not an eyewitness.

Who Is A Witness?

The WA Police Investigation Doctrine (the Doctrine) categorises witnesses into five categories:

Victim

• The person or entity upon which the offence was committed.

• Victimology.

• Proof of facts such as legal entity, age, legal status.

Eyewitness

• Directly observed the offence.

Other significant witness

• Observed an event prior to or post the offence which is classified as relevant evidence.

General witness

• Provides relevant evidence such as continuity and/or procedure.

• Provides evidence of lead up events or background information.

Expert witness

• Provides evidence of a specialist nature and is permitted by the courts to express an expert opinion.

sections.h:
PEACE MODEL FOR WITNESSES

Planning and Preparation: Review available information and establish interview aims and objectives. Identify if the witness is vulnerable and prepare and develop your plan. For example, during the planning phase the need to consider Anunga Guidelines or a responsible adult is identified.

Engage and Explain: Develop rapport, explain the interview processes and cover legislative obligations.

Account, Clarify and Challenge: Using the Free Recall or Cognitive Interview Model gain an account of events, clarify and challenge any inconsistencies. Account for new information.

Closure: Conclude the interview and address any concerns and future ground i.e. Court process, giving evidence etc.

Evaluation: Evaluate how the information obtained impacts on the investigation and also evaluate the performance of the interviewer.

There are two models utilised within the PEACE framework, for witnesses, they are the Free Recall Model and the Cognitive Interviewing Model.

The Free Recall Model is the model generally utilised to interview witnesses. Tier two trained interviewers may utilise the Cognitive Interviewing Model if the appropriate circumstances exist. The steps to conduct a Free Recall interview are outlined below. The Free Recall and Cognitive Interview Models will be covered fully later in this chapter.

Six Step Process:

Step 1 Set the scene and initiate a free recall using an open question.

Step 2 Second recall if required. Obtain sketch from witness if applicable.

Step 3 Identify interviewee topics.

Step 4 Expand on topics.

Step 5 Identify and expand on Police Agenda topics.

Step 6 Summarise, Clarify and prepare a statement.

This section provides guidelines and procedures for:

• Interviewing witnesses.

• Identifying and interviewing vulnerable witnesses.

It is designed to assist interviewers at all levels of Investigative Interviewing.

Why Interview Witnesses?

The purpose of interviewing witnesses is to ascertain the witness's account of the alleged event(s) and any other information that may assist the investigation.

Treat All Witnesses As Individuals

As an investigator, interviewing witnesses is part of your everyday business. Witnesses come from a wide range of backgrounds and each presents you with different challenges. They all have different needs and concerns and levels of involvement in the investigation. (For example, you may have a witness who is a victim of the crime or a witness with no links to the victim whatsoever but who saw what occurred). There can never be a 'one size fits all' approach to the interaction with witnesses and individual circumstances should always be taken into consideration.

Give Special Care to Victims

Treat all witnesses with fairness, empathy and sensitivity. As victims may be at a high risk of being traumatised, stressed and in fear, additional care should be taken when conducting an interview with them. It is likely the recollection of events may be traumatic and their behaviour should be monitored. Their behaviour and ability could also be impacted by physical injuries, intoxication, tiredness and so on (delay the interview if appropriate). Consider whether the witness is currently in a safe environment and the type of information the witness is likely to give.

It is paramount you consider the witness's well-being and your investigative needs in this decision making.

sections.h:
VULNERABLE WITNESSES

Definition of a Vulnerable Person

A vulnerable person for the purpose of a police interview, be that suspect or witness, is defined as someone who falls within one or more of the following categories:

• A child is always a vulnerable person.

• People with impaired intellectual function are always vulnerable.

Or people who:

• Have impaired physical functioning.

• Are Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islanders.

• Are from non-English speaking backgrounds.

• Have suffered an emotional or traumatic incident.

Which as a result, may:

• Predispose them to suggestibility.

• Impact on voluntariness.

• Complicate communications between the police and the interviewee.

Any of the above circumstances may result in flawed information being captured during the interview.

Persons fitting this definition must be afforded enhanced services to ensure fairness to the person and accuracy of the information obtained during an interview.

The following are important considerations that will impact on what provisions need to be made relating to witnesses. Decisions on who will conduct the interview, the structure, style, duration, and pace of the interview will be made based on information relevant to these issues. As the investigating officer (IO) it is paramount any factors effecting the vulnerability of a witness are identified and dealt with in line with legislation, policy and case law. If witnesses are not interviewed appropriately vital information may be missed, contaminated or not accepted by the court.

While it is not possible to supply an exhaustive list of people who may be vulnerable in the criminal justice system, the following is a list to assist in identifying vulnerability factors:

Child / juvenile.

Aboriginal people and Torres Strait Islander People.

Immaturity, either in terms of age or development.

Any infirmity, including early dementia or disease.

Mental illness.

Intellectual disability.

Illiteracy or limited education which may impair a person's capacity to understand police questions.

People who are of non-English speaking background.

Alcoholism.

Physical disabilities including deafness or loss of sight.

Drug dependence.

Cultural, ethnic or religious factors including those relating to gender attitudes.

Trauma.

It must be understood a person's vulnerability does not have to be permanent. Witnesses need to be assessed in accordance with how they present to police at the time of contact and should be supported appropriately.

Vulnerable Witnesses and Interview Considerations

Children

Definition of a child: A person who is under 18 years of age.

All children are vulnerable. The below table identifies additional vulnerable characteristics.

Children who are victims of offences listed in Schedule 7, Evidence Act should be interviewed by a Specialist Child Interviewer (SCI) from the Child Assessment and Interview Team (CAIT). Refer Annexure One at rear of this manual.

CAIT Request for Interview.

A child who is Culturally and Linguistically Diverse (CaLD) or English is their second language. An interpreter may be required (See below hyperlinks).

A child who has a physical impairment (for example, deafness, blindness etc.) that impedes their ability to understand and communicate.

A child who is Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander shall be interviewed in compliance with the Anunga Guidelines. An interpreter may be required (see below hyperlinks) if the police, responsible adult or the child identify one is necessary.

A child suffering trauma.

A child suffering a mental impairment.

Adult Witness of Aboriginal or Torres Strait Island Descent If the witness is an Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander, the witness shall be interviewed in compliance with the Anunga Guidelines. An interpreter may be required (See below hyperlink) if the police or the witness identify one is necessary. An interview friend may also be required as outlined in the Anunga Guidelines.

A Witness Who is (CaLD) or English is Their Second Language To interview a person (adult or child) who does not understand, speak or communicate in English as a first language the interviewing officer may utilise an approved interpreter or translator as outlined in the Police Manual. (Refer to Working with Interpreters).

A Witness Suffering Mental Impairment or Disability

Prior to conducting an interview with a witness with a mental impairment or a disability, the interviewing officer should assess the ability of the witness to effectively understand and participate in the interview process. If the interviewing officer identifies that the witness has no, or limited understanding of the interview process, consideration should be given for a SCI to conduct the interview. (See CAIT information under Children heading).

If the witness has a disability, consideration should be given to utilising an interpreter or a translator if it is considered one is required. (Refer to Working with Interpreters) In all other cases when interviewing a witness who is vulnerable, the principles of the Anunga Guidelines should be considered.

Multiple Witnesses

If there is more than one witness to an incident care should be taken to minimise the risk of memory contamination between witnesses by:

• Separating the witnesses as soon as possible and interviewing them individually.

• If the interview is being delayed, consider asking the witnesses not to discuss the incident with other people and explaining to them why you are taking this action, i.e. the potential for post-event information to contaminate memory.

Witnesses Who Become Suspects

If a witness becomes a suspect during an interview, procedures for interviewing suspects should be adopted as soon as the suspicion arises. Do not continue interviewing the person as a witness in the hope of eliciting admissions or confessional material. Comply with all legal, policy and case law obligations. (Refer to Suspect Investigative Interviewing Manual).

If you are going to interview a person as a witness in relation to an incident and you suspect them in anyway of being involved in the offence as an offender, you must interview them as a suspect. (Refer to Suspect Investigative Interviewing Manual).

sections.t: Planning and preparation for a witness interview
sections.h:
PLANNING AND PREPARATION FOR A WITNESS INTERVIEW

Free Recall/Cognitive Interview Models

Planning and Preparation is one of the most important phases in effective interviewing. Always plan and prepare a written interview plan, no matter what type of interview is being considered, to ensure you are ready to conduct an effective and ethical interview.

Officers are encouraged to use the Automated Interview Plan (AIP) when interviewing witnesses. It allows the interviewer to produce an interview plan tailored to the witness's vulnerabilities.

What should be covered when planning and preparing for an interview?

Step 1

Consider these factors when planning and preparing for an interview:

• Interview aims and objectives.

• Investigatively important topics.

• Witness profile: identity, criminal history, victimology.

• Legal requirements.

• Interview structure.

• Contingencies for interviewee reaction.

• Practical arrangements, including whether or not the interview should be delayed due to your witness being traumatised, intoxicated, etc.

Step 2 Decide if the witness is vulnerable. Refer to the section in this manual relating to interviewing vulnerable witnesses.

Step 3 Complete a written interview plan.

Interview Aims and Objectives

Develop a good understanding of the investigation and the purpose of the interview by:

• Examining all available, relevant information, witness statements, scene examination, physical material (PM) and other supporting documents.

• Setting the aims and objectives for the interview. These should focus on the investigatively important topics to be covered, including elements of the offence and possible defences if applicable.

Investigatively Important Topics

Identify topics needing to be explored for the investigation, including:

Circumstances of alleged offending including what happened immediately before, during and after the alleged offence.

All physical and verbal interactions between the witness and the alleged offender or anyone else.

Type and severity of alleged offence.

Elements of the offence and probable defences.

Identity and description of the suspect and other witnesses:

• If asking a witness to describe a person ask them the following question. "Describe the person in as much detail as possible."

• Explore the description topics in the order given by the witness.

• At the completion of the witnesses' description you will need to cover off on areas not addressed, for example, gender, race, age, height, build, hair (style, length, colour), skin colour, complexion, distinguishing features, clothing, carrying anything etc.

Cover ADVOKATE with witness if relevant.

Descriptions of the scene, PM and other items of interest.

sections.h:
ADVOKATE

ADVOKATE was created as a result of UK case law, R v TURNBALL 1977, in relation to mistaken identity. The guidelines set out the points significant witnesses need to cover when describing/identifying someone or something.

These points need to be covered for anything of significance, i.e. Suspect, Vehicle of Interest (VOI). It is recommended that ADVOKATE be used more than once during your interview to gain maximum detailed information.

A – Amount of time under observation.

D – Distance from the witnessed event.

V – Visibility at the time of the event.

O – Obstructions.

K – Known or seen before.

A – Any reason to remember.

T – Time since the incident.

E – Errors or discrepancies.
sections.b: ADVOKATE ADVOKATE was created as a result of UK case law, R v TURNBALL 1977, in relation to mistaken identity. The guidelines set out the points significant witnesses need to cover when describing/identifying someone or something. These points need to be covered for anything of significance, i.e. Suspect, Vehicle of Interest (VOI). It is recommended that ADVOKATE be used more than once during your interview to gain maximum detailed information. A – Amount of time under observation. D – Distance from the witnessed event. V – Visibility at the time of the event. O – Obstructions. K – Known or seen before. A – Any reason to remember. T – Time since the incident. E – Errors or discrepancies.

sections.t: Witness profile, emotional state and preparing the interview
sections.h:
WITNESS PROFILE

Build up a profile of the witness by gaining as much information as possible about:

Name and preferred name (For example, Matthew but prefers Matt).

Gender and sexuality if relevant.

Age and maturity.

Race, culture, religion and first language.

Any physical, intellectual, psychological or psychiatric impairment.

Relationship of witness to the alleged offender.

Domestic circumstances.

Current or previous contact with police.

Employment and routines.

Conduct check of police intelligence holdings (IMS, IDM etc.).

Also consider if you have identified factors affecting the vulnerability of the witness, ensure the witness is interviewed by a SCI or an interpreter or translator are used if required.

Witness: Emotional State

Consider the witness's emotional and physical state including:

Any trauma suffered.

Fears of intimidation.

Likely impact of recalling traumatic events on the witness's welfare.

Impact on behaviour of physical injuries, intoxication, tiredness and so on (delay the interview if appropriate).

Whether the witness is currently in a safe environment.

Type of evidence the witness is likely to give.

It is paramount you consider the witness's well-being and your investigative needs in this decision making.

Preparing the Interview Location

A quiet room that is free from distractions and interruptions allowing both you and the witness to concentrate.

Set up the interview room and arrange the seating.

Check equipment.

Make sure you have communication aids – including pen and paper for drawing sketch.

Consider implications if a support person or interpreter/translator is required.

Provide the witness with a drink of water and any available refreshments if required.

Preparing the Witness For Interview

The Engage and Explain interview phase may be immediately prior to the interview or on a separate occasion depending on the circumstances of the case. It can take place in person or over the telephone.

sections.h:
ENGAGE AND EXPLAIN

Engage the Witness

Establish rapport to put the witness at ease by:

Introducing yourself and any others present.

Asking the witness what they would like to be called.

Confirming the witness has a suitable amount of time for the interview.

Personalising the interview, i.e. treat the witness as an individual and talk to them in a manner and language they understand.

Discussing neutral topics.

Communicating empathy, i.e. addressing any concerns about events, the interview and the investigation.

Maintain impartiality to establish the foundation for a relationship of trust by:

• Keeping an open mind, i.e. don't pre-judge the witness.

• Using open questions and not interrupting - begin with a TEDS style question to encourage the witness to provide sufficient information.

• Actively listening.

Explain Interview Procedures

Explain interview procedures including:

Reason(s) for the interview - do this in a way that makes the interview's purpose clear.

The interview structure - tell them they will be asked for their account and then you may ask questions to clarify their information.

Summarising:

• Allows the interviewer to check understanding of what has been said and in doing so provides the interviewee with the opportunity to make any corrections.

• It shows you value what the interviewee tells you and encourages them to become increasingly open with you.

• It assists you with encoding the information into your memory through rehearsal.

• It confirms the account and ownership of the information as correct by the interviewee.

The process to be adopted - note taking and if you are not making eye contact it does not mean you are not listening.

They will need to concentrate to ensure they recall as much information as possible.

You will give them time to think to assist with the recall process.

Give an account of all they know in as much detail as possible.

Do not edit their account, let them tell you everything that comes to mind – including all the information they are not confident about or think may be trivial or partial, information they think you already know.

Take their time and set their own pace.

You want them to:

• Do most of the talking because they have information you want to know.

• Give you as much detail as possible and tell you if they don't know something.

• Not be influenced by what they think you or others might want them to say.

• Correct you if they feel you misunderstood something they said.

sections.h:
ACCOUNT, CLARIFY AND CHALLENGE

When interviewing a witness the Free Recall Model should be used in the majority of occasions. A tier two interviewing officer may utilise the Cognitive Interviewing Model if the interviewer is of the view it would assist the witness with their recall and if the circumstances are appropriate (Cooperative, non-traumatised witness). The cognitive interview is a memory facilitator, with the interviewee being in control of the information flowing from the interview.

Free Recall Model

Step 1 Set the scene and initiate a free recall using an open question.

Step 2 Second recall if required. Request a sketch if relevant.

Step 3 Identify interviewee topics.

Step 4 Expand on topics, summarise and clarify.

Step 5 Identify and expand on interviewer topics (refer to your plan).

Step 6 Summarise, Clarify and prepare statement.

• Ensure your questions are relevant and have a purpose.

• Be mindful of the tone used in questioning.

• Always open with a TEDS question.

• Probe with TEDS and/or 5W&H questions.

• Use closed questions if required or as a last resort.

• At the end of the interview ask the witness if there is anything else relevant that has not been discussed.

Cognitive Interview Model

The Cognitive Interview Model utilises the framework of the Free Recall Model. The cognitive interview primarily aims to place the witness back in the environment of the witnessed event. The process is undertaken to assist the witness to recall the event. Cognitive interviewing should only be conducted on a cooperative non-traumatised witness by a tier two trained interviewer.

The instruction used, asks the interviewee to reconstruct in their mind the context, both physical (environmental) and personal (For example, how they felt at the time) of the witnessed event.

'You witnessed an incident/event this morning, tell me everything you can, even the things you think are not important, and even if you can't remember something completely'.

(PAUSE)

'Everything that comes to mind, tell me in your own time and pace'.

Think of where you were. (PAUSE)

How are you feeling? (PAUSE)

What are you hearing? (PAUSE)

What can you smell? (PAUSE)

Think of all the people present. (PAUSE)

Think about all the objects there. Think of the layout of the (where the incident took place). (PAUSE) 'Get a really good picture in your mind and tell me everything you can remember without leaving anything out. All that pops into your mind, tell me'. (PAUSE) Note Taking

Notes of an interview are of immense value to the interviewer. They provide cues to the interviewer to use to steer the interview in a particular direction, which is of particular importance at the probing stage.

However, the notes should not be allowed to affect a free flowing interview that is already heading in the right direction. Nor should notes be taken with the intent to produce a verbatim account of what was said (That can be challenging, even for a professional stenographer). Highly detailed note taking can focus the interviewer's attention too sharply on the notes and not sharply enough on what the interviewee is saying. Where notes are concerned, in an interview context all that is required is a brief record, often just key words or topics the interviewer may want to investigate more fully.

Notes are also useful to the interviewer in terms of dividing the interviewee's account into sections that the interviewer can manage more effectively, particularly in relation to scheduling sections for further development at the probing stage of the interview. It does not matter if the notes are illegible to other people; it is important the interviewer can read and use them as an aid to memory.

The notes should be clear enough so that the interviewer can obtain cues from them quickly and without interrupting the natural pace of the interview. There are a variety of different note taking methods, including, topic boxes, timelines, mind bubbles, bullet points etc. Notes are a tool to assist the interviewer effectively remember information supplied by the interviewee.

It does not matter what method is used by the interviewer so long as the interviewer is able to decipher the notes and record sufficient information to ensure the witness statement compiled after the interview is complete and accurate.

All notes made during an interview by the interviewer or the interviewee are disclosable and will be retained in the CRIME file.

Witness Sketch

To help the interviewee to communicate with you and to recall events, give them the opportunity to draw a sketch if relevant. Remember a picture speaks a thousand words. Any sketch produced should be signed, dated and given a title by the witness. It may later be produced as an exhibit. It will assist the witness and members of the jury should the matter come to trial.

Keep the sketch available for review and reference as required throughout the interview. Any sketches made during an interview by the interviewer or the interviewee are disclosable and will be retained in the CRIME file.

sections.h:
CLOSURE

Closing Interviews

At the completion of the interview ensure the following points have been covered:

• Review information obtained and confirm all topics have been covered by referring to your interview plan.

• Ask if the witness has any questions and answer them appropriately.

• Thank the witness for their time and effort.

• Request that if they recall further information about the event after the interview, to contact you so an additional interview can be arranged.

• Prepare for future events (e.g. referral to support services, photographs, medical examination, court or further police involvement with the witness).

• Provide them with a card including your name and contact details.

• Ensure victim support service details are provided to the witness.
sections.b: CLOSURE Closing Interviews At the completion of the interview ensure the following points have been covered: • Review information obtained and confirm all topics have been covered by referring to your interview plan. • Ask if the witness has any questions and answer them appropriately. • Thank the witness for their time and effort. • Request that if they recall further information about the event after the interview, to contact you so an additional interview can be arranged. • Prepare for future events (e.g. referral to support services, photographs, medical examination, court or further police involvement with the witness). • Provide them with a card including your name and contact details. • Ensure victim support service details are provided to the witness.

sections.h:
EVALUATE

As soon as practical after the interview, consider:

• The impact of the information on the investigation.

• What evidence there is in relation to offences, elements of the offence and potential defences.

• Descriptions of people, items and events that may be vital to the investigation, the urgency and need for further enquiries.

Prior to asking the witness to sign their prepared statement ask a colleague or supervisor to proof read the statement to ensure all relevant information has been obtained and adequately covered.

Consider what follow-up action is required to prepare the witness for any future court proceedings. It may be advantageous to keep in regular contact with the witness especially as court proceedings approach.

Self-Evaluation

Self-evaluation is an important part of professional development. Through critical review of your performance you can identify areas of improvement that may lead to more professional collection of evidence.

Self-evaluate your own performance:

What did you do well?

What could you have done better?

What areas can you develop?

How will you acquire these skills?

ADVICE

For advice on investigative interviewing related matters contact the Detective Training School or the Investigative Standards Unit.

sections.h:
ANNEXURE ONE

Anunga Rules

AD-01.00 Attachment B - Anunga Rules

Judgement Delivered By Chief Justice Forster

On 30 April, 1979, Mr. Justice FORSTER handed down his reasons for rejecting typewritten records of conversations in the matter of QUEEN v Angus ANUNGA, Sandy AJAX, Clancy AJAX, TJINGUNYA, Nari WHEELER and Frankie Miller JAGAMARA in the Northern Territory Supreme Court in August, 1975. (R v Anunga (1976) 11 ALR 412) Inter alia Mr. Justice FORSTER said:

"It seems to me now, however, to be important that the Court should put on record general guidelines for the conduct of Police Officers when interrogating Aboriginal persons.

I preface this statement of guidelines by pointing out that Aboriginal people often do not understand English very well and that, even if they do understand the words, they may not understand the concepts which English phrases and sentences express. Even with the use of interpreters this problem is by no means solved. Police and legal English sometimes is not translatable into the Aboriginal language at all and there are no separate Aboriginal words for some simple words like "in", "at", "on", "by", "with", or "over", these being suffixes added to the word they qualify. Some words may translate literally into Aboriginal language but mean something different. "Did you go into his house?" means to an English-speaking person, "did you go into the building?", but to an Aboriginal it may also mean, "Did you go within the fence surrounding the house?" English concepts of time, number and distance are imperfectly understood if at all by Aboriginal people, many of the more primitive of whom cannot tell the time by a clock. One frequently hears the answer "long time", which depending on the context may be minutes, hours, days, weeks, or years. In case I may be misunderstood, I should also emphasise that I am not expressing the view that Aboriginal people are any less intelligent than white people but simply that their concepts of certain things and the terms in which they are expressed may be wholly different to those of white people.

Another matter which needs to be understood is that most Aboriginal people are basically courteous and polite and will answer questions by white people in the way in which they think the questioner wants. Even if they are not courteous and polite there is the same reaction when they are dealing with an authority figure such as a policeman. Indeed their action is probably a combination of natural politeness and their attitude to someone in authority. Some Aboriginal people find the standard caution quite bewildering, even if they understand that they do not have to answer questions, because if they do not have to answer questions, then why are the questions being asked?

Guidelines to be Followed when Interviewing Aboriginals Bearing in mind these preliminary observations which are based partly upon my own knowledge and partly by evidence I have heard in numerous cases I lay down the following guidelines. They apply, of course, to persons who are being questioned as suspects.

1. When an Aboriginal person is being interrogated as a suspect, unless he is as fluent in English as the average white man of English descent, an interpreter able to interpret in and from the Aboriginal person's language should be present, and his assistance should be utilised whenever necessary to ensure complete and mutual understanding.

2. When an Aboriginal is being interrogated it is desirable where practicable that a "prisoner's friend" (who may also be the interpreter) be present. The "prisoner's friend", should be someone in whom the Aboriginal has apparent confidence. He may be a mission or settlement superintendent or a member of the staff of one of these institutions who knows and is known to the Aboriginal. He may be a station owner, manager or overseer or an officer from the Department of Aboriginal Affairs. The combinations of persons and situations are variable and the categories of persons I have mentioned are not exclusive. The important thing is that the "prisoner's friend", be someone in whom the Aboriginal has confidence, by whom he will feel supported.

3. Great care should be taken in administering the caution when it is appropriate to do so. It is simply not adequate to administer it in the usual terms and say, "Do you understand that?" or "Do you understand you do not have to answer questions?" Interrogating Police Officers, having explained the caution in simple terms, should ask the Aboriginal to tell them what is meant by the caution, phrase by phrase, and should not proceed with the interrogation until it is clear the Aboriginal has apparent understanding of his right to remain silent. Most experienced police officers in the Territory already do this. The problem of the caution is a difficult one but the presence of a "prisoner's friend" or interpreter and adequate and simple questioning about the caution should go a long way towards solving it.

4. Great care should be taken in formulating questions so that so far as possible the answer which is wanted or expected is not suggested in any way. Anything in the nature of cross-examination should be scrupulously avoided as answers to it have no probative value. It should be borne in mind that it is not only the wording of the question, which may suggest the answer, but also the manner and tone of voice which are used.

5. Even when an apparent frank and free confession has been obtained relating to the commission of an offence, police should continue to investigate the matter in an endeavour to obtain proof of the commission of the offence from other sources. Failure to do this, among other things, led to the rejection of confessional records of interview in the cases of Nari WHEELER and Frank JAGAMARA.

6. Because Aboriginal people are often nervous and ill at ease in the presence of white authority figures like policemen, it is particularly important that they be offered a meal, if they are being interviewed in a Police Station, or in the company of Police or in custody when a meal time arrives. They should always be offered a drink of water. They should be asked if they wish to use the lavatory if they are in the company of Police or under arrest.

7. It is particularly important that Aboriginal and other people are not interrogated when they are disabled by illness or drunkenness or tiredness. Admissions so gained will probably be rejected by a Court. Interrogation should not continue for an unreasonably long time.

8. Should an Aboriginal seek legal assistance reasonable steps should be taken to obtain such assistance. If an Aboriginal states he does not wish to answer further questions or any questions the interrogation should not continue.

9. When it is necessary to remove clothing for forensic examination or for the purposes of medical examination, steps must be taken forthwith to supply substitute clothing."

Should an Aboriginal seek legal assistance reasonable steps should be taken to obtain such assistance. If an Aboriginal states he does not wish to answer further questions or any questions the interrogation should not continue. 9. When it is necessary to remove clothing for forensic examination or for the purposes of medical examination, steps must be taken forthwith to supply substitute clothing."

sections.t: Annexure Three — Section 10 Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (Interpreters)
sections.h:
ANNEXURE THREE

Section 10 Criminal Investigation Act 2006

Interpreters

Criminal Investigation Act (CIA) 2006 s.10 - Informing people who do not understand English.

If under this Act (CIA) an officer is required to inform a person about any matter and the person is for any reason unable to understand or communicate in spoken English sufficiently, the officer must, if it is practical to do so in the circumstances, use an interpreter or other qualified person or other means to inform the person about the matter.

Identify the country they come from and the exact language they speak (For example, a Chinese person may speak Mandarin or Cantonese.;

Arrange for an interpreter using the contracted interpreting service. Provide the interpreter with an outline of the incident and the reason for the interview.

Ascertain whether the interpreter is an appropriate person to assist. They must be able to write and speak the suspect or witness's language fluently and be impartial and independent to the investigation. This means they must have no prior knowledge of the suspect or witnesses involved.

If they know any of the parties involved in the investigation (including the suspect), they should only be used in exceptional circumstances, i.e. no one else is available and the interview cannot be delayed. The extent of the connection should also be taken into account when making this decision. If you use an interpreter who knows the parties involved, make sure you keep a record of your rationale.

Record the interpreter's qualifications and contact details or those of their organisation.

Prepare for the interview in the usual manner.

When the interpreter arrives:

• Allow the interpreter the opportunity to brief both you and the witness on their professional role and how they will conduct themselves;

• If necessary, inform the interpreter their role is to interpret your questions and the witness's answers back to you;

• Their interpretation should be as direct as possible in 'first person'. They should not enter into general discussion with the witness.

Make sure the interpreter understands the Section 28 CIA rights. Instruct them to clearly explain these to the witness to ensure the witness understands.

Consider seeking advice from the interpreter about any communication issues that may arise.

Consider the interpreter's needs for breaks during the interview.

Using the interpreter, complete the usual Engage and Explain process as you would for any other witness. Where appropriate, inform the witness of their rights with the aid of the interpreter.

While video recording the interview:

• Ask the interpreter to introduce themselves, explain their role, experience and qualifications. (They may later be required to give evidence in respect of their knowledge and experience).

• Explain the interpreter's role as detailed above.

• Ensure the interpreter is clearly visible during the interview.

Do not speak in segments. Let the interpreter control the length of each segment and signal to you when to stop.

Complete the account phase using an appropriate interview model. Your questions are interpreted to the witness in their language and their answers interpreted back to you.

For safety and impartiality reasons make sure the interpreter leaves the room with you if you take any interview breaks.

Complete the closure process as usual.

Before the interpreter leaves, ensure you have their full contact details (or that of their agency).

sections.h:
PURPOSE

The Investigation Training Précis are maintained by the Investigative Standards Unit. The précis have been written to offer guidance and assistance to all members of the Western Australian Police Force (WA Police Force) in relation to police investigations.

The précis are written to highlight the theory and methodology underpinning 'Criminal Investigations' and are not intended to be referenced as Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).

Should information regarding SOPs be sought then readers should refer to the 'Investigation Standards' link located on the WA Police Force computer desktop.

The précis are an 'all of agency' resource but readers should be aware they are specifically written for Investigator Training.

sections.h:
INTRODUCTION

A statement is the product of the interview process. Investigators should remember when interviewing a witness the primary objective is to obtain a comprehensive account with the secondary objective being to record the account in a statement. A good statement will only ever be possible by conducting a quality interview utilising recognised 'Investigative Interviewing' techniques. Conversely, a poor interview will result in a poor quality statement.

For further information, refer to the 'Investigative Standards' website under the 'Investigative Interviewing' tab.

Additionally a witness statement can be used by the investigation team to better inform investigators to plan and strategise investigative actions. This subject addresses the methodology that should be utilised by investigators to prepare, formulate and structure quality statements to best reflect the potential evidence to be provided by a witness.

Statements are a key method of recording the information and evidence obtained as a result of witness related investigative actions under the Five Key Investigative Strategies (The 5KIS).

sections.t: Witness statement and the five witness categories
sections.h:
WITNESS STATEMENT

A statement is a guide for the prosecution and defence detailing the oral testimony a witness may give. It captures all of the potentially relevant oral testimony that a witness will give at trial. The purpose of the witness statement is to set out the evidence of the witness prior to the trial commencing so that all parties involved in the trial have an appreciation of the situation. Therefore the statement must fully reflect all the potential evidence that the witness can (or cannot) provide to the court. This will ensure any decision to conduct the trial or to enter a plea of guilty will be fully informed and based on the totality of the available evidence.

The construction of a witness statement is a relatively simple process but there are many nuances that need to be adhered to, in order for the finished product to form a professional document that may be presented before the court.

The WA Police Investigation Doctrine identifies five categories of witnesses (see below). Witnesses come in many forms and each is as important as the other during the trial process. It is the testimony of all witnesses as a collective that reconstruct the offence and subsequent investigation within the confines of the court. Therefore, investigators should maintain professional standards for all statements prepared from all witnesses. It is possible a witness may fall into a number of categories dependent on the circumstances of the offence and this includes police officers. Where this occurs investigators should ensure the statement meets the expectations of each respective category.

Victim

The content of the victim's statement will depend on the circumstances of the offence(s) and may include proof of facts such as legal entity, age, legal status, consent and/or all of the elements. Investigators should ensure they are fully conversant with the elements of the offence(s) under investigation and where required are addressed in the statement accordingly.

Eye Witnesses

Eyewitness testimony is generally presumed to be the best type of evidence. This is typically because the witness can give evidence of what they saw, heard, smelt, touched or tasted whilst the offence was actually occurring.

There have been numerous studies into witnesses. One study involved an experiment in which subjects acted as jurors in a criminal case. Jurors heard a description of a robbery-murder, then a prosecution argument, then an argument for the defence. Some jurors heard only circumstantial evidence, others heard from a clerk who claimed to identify the defendant. In the first case, 18% percent found the defendant guilty, but in the second, 72% found the defendant guilty.

Although studies indicate that eye witness testimony carries a lot of weight with jurors, eye witness testimony is often flawed, this can occur because of a person's faulty observation and recollection, a person's bias or due to the witness lying. If several people witness a crime it is probative to look for similarities in their collective descriptions to substantiate the facts of an event, keeping in mind the contrasts of individual descriptions. This illustrates the point that investigators should not stop the search for witnesses just because they have identified one eye witness.

The above illustrates the importance of ensuring an eye witness account is accurately recorded in statement form and clearly articulates how and what they actually were able to observe (by one or more of the five senses) in relation to the offence(s). Potential sources of corroboration should also be recorded in the statement, this could include 'who they were with at the time' or possibly documentary evidence e.g. phone records.

Other Significant Witnesses

'Other Significant Witness' is a person who has observed (by one or more of the five senses) events which are relevant before or after the actual offence. Examples include the clothing worn by a suspect, observing the suspect to leave a house after they have committed an offence, observing a suspect to play with a knife before the offence, police officer interviewing the suspect etc. Therefore it is important the witness statement reflects how they observed these events but also articulates that they did not see the actual offence.

Expert Witnesses

Opinions, inferences and beliefs of ordinary individuals are irrelevant in a court of law. The opinions of experts are admissible if it is based on their area of expertise. An expert witness or professional witness is a person who, by virtue of their education, training, skill, or experience, is believed to have knowledge in a particular subject beyond that of the average person. It is considered this knowledge is sufficient that others may legally rely upon the witness's specialised opinion about evidence or a fact in issue within the scope of their expertise and this is referred to as expert opinion.

Typically, experts are relied upon for opinions on cause of death, severity of injury, degree of insanity, comparisons of items, scientific experiments and the like. The expert has a heavy responsibility, especially in trials.

The use of expert witnesses is sometimes criticised because they are often used by both sides to advocate differing positions, and it is left up to a jury or laymen to decide which expert witness to believe. Sometimes one side will utilise an expert witness to provide junk science testimony in order to convince a jury.

It is for these reasons that investigators should ensure that the expert witness's statement clearly details:

1. The qualifications and experience of the person making the statement.

2. The relevance of the person's qualification or experience to the fact in issue.

3. The processes used to establish the findings.

4. What the person's expert opinion is in relation to the fact in issue.

General Witnesses

'General Witness' is a person who provides relevant evidence with regard to continuity and/or procedure. They may also give evidence of background information which assists with building a context for the offence(s). It is important the witness statement clearly reflects the role of the witness or articulates they are providing background information only. This type of evidence is often admissible under 'Res Gestae' the principle of completeness. An example of a general witness is a bank manager simply providing documents to police to support the commission of an offence.

sections.h:
A statement, where possible, should include points that can corroborate the witnesses account.

If the witness saw an offence and then acted in a particular way i.e. phoned the police, then the calling of the police should be detailed in the statement. The telephone records will provide independent support of their evidence regarding the time of the offence.

This corroboration will increase the witness's credibility.

sections.h:
INTERVIEW TO STATEMENT PROCESS

Investigators should ensure they are able to transition from interview phase to statement phase without compromising the quality of either process. To assist, the following process should be undertaken:

• Conduct the interview in accordance with the Investigative Interviewing methodology. Notes should be taken during this phase.

• Complete a draft of the statement (handwritten or electronic).

• Check accuracy of statement content with interviewee and notes taken during the interview phase.

• Peer review for evidentiary content and grammatical accuracy.

• Complete final draft and have the interviewee read it. Any changes requested by the interviewee must be amended accordingly.

• Interviewee signs, times and dates each page (adoption) as does the officer witnessing the signature.

Any changes requested by the interviewee must be amended accordingly. • Interviewee signs, times and dates each page (adoption) as does the officer witnessing the signature.

sections.h:
EVIDENTIARY CONSIDERATIONS

All relevant admissible evidence given by a witness should be recorded within the statement. Investigators must familiarise themselves with the elements of the offence they are investigating. It is self-evident that a witness may be able to supply information about one or all elements of an offence, therefore knowledge of the elements will enable the investigator to address them in the statement.

Evidence is relevant when it can be regarded as affecting, directly or indirectly, the probability of the occurrence (or non-occurrence) or existence (or non-existence) of one or more facts in issue. The statement should contain all of the witness's relevant knowledge of the event. Any variation between a statement and the testimony given by the witness will invariably result in cross-examination by the defence and it may, in some circumstances, affect the witness's credibility.

The statement should read in a chronological order, this is because the testimony of the witness will in most cases be chronological. A jury will also be able to follow a chronological story better than one that jumps from event to event and back again.

Descriptions play a big part in the identification of an accused or other persons involved in an offence. All descriptions in a statement need to be as detailed as the witness can recall. This will make any subsequent formal identification more credible, reduce faulty identifications and subject the evidence to less scrutiny by the court.

Description is not restricted to physical description, but includes the clothing described by the witness. Investigators are reminded that in the case of a sexual offence, a detailed description of the victims clothing is essential.

Hearsay Evidence

Hearsay evidence is a complex matter and will inevitably result in detailed legal argument. Investigators are reminded that hearsay evidence should not be included in the statement unless it falls within the category of exceptions to the Hearsay Rules. Hearsay evidence is evidence of a statement made other than by a witness, who was present at the scene or incident, who perceived what occurred by one of his five senses – i.e. It is evidence of what someone else did or noticed.

Hearsay evidence exceptions include, but are not limited to:

• Admissions.

• Evidence of early complaint.

• Statements made through interpreters.

• Dying Declarations.

• Some declarations regarding documents.

• Certain statements made under 'Res Gestae', the principle of completeness.

With matters of hearsay evidence investigators should be guided by relevance rather than legal admissibility. The accompanying inset gives direction on hearsay evidence.

sections.h:
To be admissible (unless excluded by any other rule) information must be relevant. When making your decision, ask yourself, does this information directly relate to the offence the accused is charged with?

Police officers often conduct a number of enquires due to procedural guidelines and practices etc. but the specific duty or action does not always have to be included in your statement.

It should however, be recorded elsewhere for future reference if needed or if it becomes relevant. E.g. Running sheet.

sections.h:
In providing a brief to the ODPP or to Police Prosecutions, the objective must at all times, be to provide all relevant information pertinent to the allegations.

The issue of what represents hearsay evidence is a complex one and the admissibility of that evidence will often be the subject of legal argument.

Officers should be guided by principles of relevance rather than legal admissibility and if there is uncertainty as to relevance of information, that information should be included in witness statements.

In the event an officer considers that the information is not appropriate for inclusion in a witness statement but yet may be useful information for the prosecution or defence, that information should be included in an additional report placed on the brief and addressed to the Prosecutor.

sections.t: Content and style of a statement
sections.h:
CONTENT AND STYLE OF A STATEMENT

The information contained within a statement will result from the interview with the witness and the application of Investigative Interviewing techniques will ensure that all relevant information is captured.

It is not the intent of this subject to regurgitate that which is supplied within the subject of investigative interviewing, however there are several points that need to be raised.

A witness statement must contain truthful and reliable evidence, according to the clear recollection and personal experience of the witness. Its contents should tell the prosecutor and defence what the witness recalls detailed in distinguishable topic areas (paragraphs). The topic areas will assist the court to explore these topic areas in a logical manner and avoid confusing the witness evidence.

A witness statement is a story told by the witness about a series of events that the witness experienced in the past. For the most part these events can be described in the words of the witness by using reference to time, place and the experience.

Each event is part of the witness's personal experience. The description of the event should be what the witness saw, said, heard or did. When another person forms part of the experience of the witness then the description of the event should refer to what that other was heard to say or seen to do. The rules of hearsay need to be considered when the conversation is relayed.

A witness statement that is prepared in this way becomes a written version of what otherwise might be the script for a film of the described events. If an event cannot be imagined as image or sound then it is likely that the witness statement is not in the correct form.

The witness should be told by the interviewer to only include in their statement what they recollect and know to be true. That knowledge comes from recollection of personal experience of events and the witness should be confident about that recollection. The witness should not speculate or guess. If they are not confident about whether an event occurred then this should be detailed in the statement i.e. "John came over Friday night. I'm not sure what time it was or if he came with anyone, but I know it was Friday night because I was watching Friday Night Football".

Correct Tense

Most evidence concerns events that occurred in the past. Witness statements should therefore use past and not present tense. The problem of incorrect use of present tense often arises when a witness describes a scene, or talks about a relationship between things, system, practice, or state of affairs. The use of present tense disguises the true evidence about the state of affairs at the relevant time.

Formatting Conversation

Frequently witness statements set out conclusions about the nature or the subject of what was said rather than state the words used. This is not evidence. Usually it is a conclusion about or evaluation of personal experience that has not been set out in the statement. The specified substance of the words spoken constitutes the evidence.

When including conversation into a statement then the "I said. He said." format must be used.

Conversation that is not relevant can be paraphrased such as:

I then had a discussion with the accused about …

The above example is sufficient when the conversation is clearly between yourself and another person that is easily identifiable and cannot be confused with any other person.

If the witness is unsure of the exact wording used by a person, then it should be prefaced with "I am unsure of the exact words but he said something like ..."

In some cases, there are more than two people present during a conversation and confusion may result as to "who said what?" in the statement. When this occurs it is important to specify and indicate the speaker.

Language

The language used in the statement should reflect the way people talk and not legalistic equivalents. People "see" rather than "observe" things. They "say" rather than "inform" or "advise". They "read" and do not "peruse" and they "send", "post", "fax" or "email" rather than "forward" documents. The witness "went" rather than "proceeded" somewhere.

A statement is taken by speaking with a witness and then typing it, therefore sufficient notes must be taken during the interview to ensure there is no misinterpretation of witness's evidence. Where necessary, explain any terminology used. If the witness uses a term that is unfamiliar or which may be ambiguous then an explanation of that term should be requested and included in the statement.

Do not use abbreviations within the statement, the use of abbreviations can lead to confusion and misinterpretation. There are a number of abbreviations that have different meanings within different contexts.

As can be seen in the inset there are numerous meanings for the same abbreviation, the reader of a statement may not be familiar with a specific abbreviation and this may lead to confusion which could completely change the context of the statement from the readers' perspective. Unless the abbreviation is universally recognised, for example "i.e."

Use Active Voice

Most sentences should identify the witness and if another person is involved should identify that person. The sentence should then say what the relevant individuals saw, said, heard or did. The witness may however be revealed by the context provided by the statement.

Avoid Passive Voice

Passive expressions that simply describe an event without identifying the individuals concerned should be avoided. For example "The side of the house was where the man came from" does not reveal anything about what the witness experienced. The sentence should reveal that the witness saw the man come from the side of the house. Unless it is clear from the context the form should be "I saw the man walk from the side of the house".

Avoid Collective Expressions

The individual experience of the witness is usually relevant and not some form of collective or corporate experience. The witness should talk about what 'I' saw, did, said or heard rather than what 'we' or some corporate entity saw, did, said or heard.

Likewise where another individual said or did something the individual should be identified and not 'they' or the name of some corporate or collective entity. Corporations do not talk or act, only people do and hence they must be identified. A sentence that links conduct to a corporation is a conclusion about whom the individual represented rather than a statement about what happened.

It is rarely helpful or admissible to state in a witness statement what 'we agreed' or what 'we discussed'. These are expressions of conclusions from words spoken that have not been stated.

Avoid the State of Mind

In most cases the state of mind of the witness is not relevant. It may be relevant where an allegation is made that there was a common intention in a contractual context that was not correctly recorded in the final written contract and a claim is made for rectification. It might also be relevant in the context of foreseeability and the actual knowledge of the defendant when an allegation of negligence is made. In most cases however the state of mind of the witness is not relevant. It is not a description of the personal experience of the witness. It is not what the witness saw, said, did or heard.

There are numerous expressions that reveal an irrelevant state of mind and should not be included in witness statements. They include 'I understood', 'I thought' or 'I intended'.

Avoid Interpreting Documents

A related problem is interpretation of a document. In most cases what the words used in a document mean is an issue to be decided by the court. The author's interpretation is not admissible. A sentence in the form 'When I wrote this letter I meant in paragraph 2 ...' is not likely to be admissible. The same applies to conversations. 'When I said to her [words spoken] I meant ...' and like forms should not be used. Again, interpretation usually is not a description of what the witness saw, said, heard or did.

Avoid Repeating Contents of Documents

In most cases, it is not necessary to repeat the contents of a document identified by the witness. It is sufficient for the witness to say that a document was prepared and what was done with it or that a document was received and read and then acted on and to describe the action. If the content of the document is admissible then the document should be and can be annexed as a potential exhibit. Sometimes a portion of the content of the document might be appropriately inserted in the witness statement if there is some good reason to do that; for example, to emphasise a particular point. Repetition of large slabs of document content should be avoided.

• Skidaway Institute of Oceanography, Siouan Languages, Surety Information Office, Social Investment Organization, Standard Installation Organization, Scalable Input/Output Initiative, Students Islamic Organization of India.

sections.h:
STATEMENT FORMAT

The WA Police Force have a standard format for the construction of statements. All statements must be prepared on the approved template.

Statements are to be compiled using the right half of the page only, as this allows space for the prosecutor to enter extensive notes on the left half.

Computer generated statements must be constructed using 12 point 'ARIAL' font, line spacing should be formatted at 1.5 and each paragraph is to be numbered. Margins at the top and bottom of the document are to 3 centimetres. Pages are to be numbered.

The name of the witness should be at the top of each page, the first page should read:

(Witnesses' Name)

States.

Subsequent pages should read:

Statement of (Witnesses' Name) continued.

Statement Introduction

The first paragraph of the witness's statement should include a brief introduction. This introduction should contain the witness's age, their occupation and the suburb they live in.

The witness's actual address should not be included on the statement, unless the address is an evidentiary factor such as in the case of a burglary where an element of the offence is 'enters a place of another' and as such the address becomes a fact that must be proved.

A police statement will also include an introduction. Traditionally police statements only included rank and work location, however with the introduction of educational qualifications and in line with the professionalising of investigators it is appropriate to include the investigators' qualifications in the introductions.

Police statements should also include the investigator's role within the investigation. These roles will vary but may include such things as investigating officer, exhibits officer, video operator. Complex investigation roles will include Senior Investigating Officer, Forensic Liaison Officer and Family Liaison Officer.

The introduction of the statement sets the scene for the jury or judge for the testimony which will follow in the body of the statement.

Body of the Statement

The statement should always be written in the first-person. First-person is a narrative mode in which a story is narrated by only one character that explicitly refers to him or herself using words and phrases involving 'I' (referred to as the first-person singular). This style of writing ensures that the witness only indicates the things that they have experienced with one of their five senses.

The statement should be constructed using concise paragraphs to detail the points which are relevant to the matter under investigation. The use of concise paragraphs does not mean a reduction in the detail, it simply means that long expansive pieces of information should be structured clear sub-events.

Paragraphs should not be split over two pages, this makes the statement look unprofessional and in some circumstances could lead to allegations that part of the paragraph had been changed to alter the context.

Most statements will at some point refer to a time and this should be formatted in the 12 hour clock. The 24 hour clock format should not be used. Juries comprise members of the public and as such many will not be familiar with the 24 hour clock format. Confusion on behalf of the jury regarding times may have a monumental impact on the trial.

sections.h:
MARGINAL NOTES

Throughout many statements there will be a need to include marginal notes to notify the prosecutor of the introduction of an exhibit or the identification of the accused.

Exhibit

If the witness makes reference to any 'real evidence' in their statement the word 'EXHIBIT' is to be placed on the left-hand side of the page opposite the place or places in the statement where a reference is made to the exhibit. This does not only occur when the witness is tendering an exhibit but should occur at any point the first time an exhibit is referred to, for each individual item. This will prompt the prosecutor to show the exhibit to the witness and have it either tendered or marked for identification.

Marked for identification is a process where an exhibit is shown to witnesses involved in the trial and the witness indicates that is the exhibit to which they are referring, the exhibit is not tendered in evidence until the chain of custody is complete and the witness that had last possession of the exhibit gives evidence. The statement should include a detailed description of the exhibit including, if applicable serial numbers and model numbers. A minimal description of the exhibit could lead to confusion or legal challenge during the trial. The most detailed description a witness can give for every exhibit is important.

The statement must also address what interaction the witness had with the exhibit, such as how the witness came to be in possession of the exhibit and what was done with it at the completion of dealing with it. This information will assist in constructing the chain of custody.

A document that is to be tendered through a witness should be explained by reference to the personal experience of the witness. The relevance of the document needs to be explained to ensure that's it importance is conveyed to the jury.

Identify

Insert 'IDENTIFY' on the left-hand side of the page opposite the place or places in the statement where a reference is made to an offender that has been categorically identified by name. This does not include when a witness that sees an offender and the offender is subsequently identified.

In a police statement the 'IDENTIFY' marginal note should again be when the offender is categorically identified by name and not by description. This does not include the first instance the police associate the name with an offence. Once the suspect has been identified by name in the police statement he/she is to then be referred to as the accused thereafter.

sections.h:
Role Description

…The role of the Exhibits Officer is to receive and number each exhibit seized, record a description of the item, the details of the locating officer and where the item was located.

Property Example

During the search I was handed a black handled knife, the details of which were recorded onto Exhibit Log B12345 as item BWJ1.

… later I conveyed the item to the office and recorded it onto the police IMS system.

…The item was then secured in the property store.

Drug Example

During the search I was handed a clear plastic bag which contained a crystal like substance. I placed it into security movement envelope W0012345 and recorded it onto Exhibit Log number B12345 as item BWJ1.

…I later attended the office where I recorded it onto the police Information Management System (IMS). I also opened Security Movement Envelope W0012345 and weighed the contents. I replaced the contents into Security Movement Envelope W0012346. I placed the Security Movement Envelope into the Secure Drug Storage Area.

…On the 01/01/2010 I conveyed Security Movement Envelope W0012346 to the Drug Receival Unit and handed it to Senior Constable Smith.

sections.h:
Statement Conclusion

The conclusion of the statement should outline the final piece of information that witness has to offer the court. A witness statement may include the circumstances that caused the witness to supply a statement i.e. I then attend at the Wanneroo Police Station and gave this statement.

A Police Statement in which the investigator prefers a charge may need to state the final dealings they had with the accused i.e. At the conclusion of the interview, the present charge was preferred and whether the accused was arrested, charged and bailed, or was charged by summons. It is not necessary to state that the accused was lodged at the lock-up.

Investigators should be cautious as to how they conclude the statement and avoid terminology such as 'the accused refused to participate in a video record of interview'. This type of statement has been deemed to be prejudicial to the accused as the jury may infer some guilt as to why an innocent person would refuse to talk to police.

sections.h:
DECLARATIONS ON WITNESS STATEMENTS

The Criminal Procedures Act mandates that a declaration must be placed at the end of every statement that is made by a person who is twelve years of age or over.

The Criminal Procedures Act outlines that the declaration complies with the act if it contains words to the effect of:

This statement is true to the best of my knowledge and belief. I have made this statement knowing that, if it is tendered in evidence, I will be guilty of a crime if I have wilfully included in the statement anything that I know to be false or that I do not believe is true.

To comply with this schedule of the act the WA Police have adopted the following as the standard jurat to be contained within a witness statement:

This statement is true to the best of my knowledge and belief. I have made this statement knowing that, if it is tendered in evidence, I will be guilty of a crime if I have wilfully included in the statement anything that I know to be false or that I do not believe is true.

Where the maker of a statement is a child of less than twelve years of age then the following declaration should be contained within the statement:

I know what the truth is and what lies are. Everything I have said today is the truth and is what happened to me. I have not told any lies. I know it is wrong to tell lies.

Where the statement is made by a person who cannot read, it should be read aloud to them by a person whom the witness nominates (not the interviewing officer), before they sign it. It is accompanied by a declaration of the person who read the statement to the effect that it was so read.

The following declaration should be included after the official declaration:

I declare that this statement was read aloud to me whereupon I agree that its contents are true and correct and that the statement is in compliance with the above mentioned declaration.

It is the accepted practice in Western Australia that the declaration forms the last paragraph of the statement. Ensure the declaration is presented in its entirety and not as a split paragraph.

Immediately following the declaration the maker of the statement and a witness must adopt the statement.

sections.h:
Adoption

Adoption is crucial for the legal admissibility of the statement and care in this area will ensure that the witness statement is not the subject of legal argument during the court case. The investigator must ensure that the person making the statement adopts it as being a true and accurate record of events.

Prior to the statement being adopted, the witness must be given the opportunity to read the statement and cause any amendment they wish to be made.

Every witness statement must be adopted by the maker and witnessed:

• For handwritten and typed hard copy statements the maker must sign and date the bottom of every page observed by the witness, who then signs and dates the bottom of every page. On the last page further witness details such as where, time, date and who need to be completed.

• An electronic statement via the Statement Generator App can be completed with electronic signatures appearing on the last page only.

Both comply with the Schedule 3 provisions of the Criminal Procedure Act 2004. The difference in adoption is due to the electronic statement initial pages are subject to PDF and cannot be changed whereas the hand written or typed hard copy initial pages can be amended and substituted if unsigned.

The final page of all statements must also include the suburb the statement was taken. If the witnessing officer's name is not clearly evident from their signature, then they should legibly write their name along with their signature.

It is most important that the officer taking a witness statement can identify the statement in court using their signature as a verification method. It is important an officer can demonstrate the witness was fully aware of the requirement to tell the truth during the interview and that the statement accurately represents their knowledge of the event(s). The correct adoption procedure and inclusion of the jurat are key methods of demonstrating the above.

Police statements are adopted in the same way as other witness statements and reinforce that a police witness is treated equally before the law.

At the completion of the statement the witness should be offered a copy of the statement as a matter of courtesy and professional conduct.

All copies of the statement including handwritten statements shall reflect the above mentioned adoption process.
